edition_note: Modern edition (Eerdmans, 1941 reprint); original Dutch work 'Het Werk van den Heiligen Geest' published 1888–1889, English translation 1900, predates the ISBN system.
fb_summary: Noticing how little had been written about the Holy Spirit compared to the Father and the Son, Abraham Kuyper set out to fill that gap. Originally published as short weekly articles, The Work of the Holy Spirit explores in three parts how the Spirit sanctifies both the church as a whole and the individual believer, applying Christ's finished work to human hearts. Written in accessible language for ordinary readers rather than only scholars, it remains a thorough and much-loved study of the Spirit's often-overlooked ministry.

title: By Searching
edition_note: Original OMF (China Inland Mission) edition, 1957.
fb_summary: Isobel Kuhn's By Searching tells the honest story of her own journey from childhood faith through university-years doubt and rebellion, to a crisis point after a broken engagement when she prayed, "If You will prove to me that You are, and if You will give me peace, I will give You my whole life." God answered — and Isobel went on to serve for decades as a missionary with the China Inland Mission among the Lisu people. Her testimony speaks candidly to anyone wrestling with doubt on the way to genuine faith.

title: Green Leaf in Drought
edition_note: Modern OMF International edition; recounts the escape of the last China Inland Mission missionaries from Communist China.
fb_summary: Green Leaf in Drought tells the remarkable true story of Arthur and Wilda Mathews, the last China Inland Mission missionaries to leave Communist China, who endured two and a half years of house arrest, isolation, and want before finally being allowed to depart in 1953. Taking its title and theme from Jeremiah 17:8 — the righteous flourishing like a tree by the water even in drought — the book shows how the Mathews family kept trusting God through prolonged hardship, becoming, in their weakness, a quiet witness to persecuted Chinese believers around them.

title: How Should We Then Live? The Rise and Decline of Western Thought and Culture
edition_note: Original edition published by Fleming H. Revell, 1976; no verifiable ISBN was found via search for the original or a specific current edition.
fb_summary: Francis Schaeffer's How Should We Then Live? traces the rise and decline of Western thought and culture from ancient Rome to the twentieth century, showing how a society's view of truth and God shapes its art, philosophy, science, and law. Schaeffer argues that when a culture abandons belief in the God who is truly there, it eventually loses any solid basis for meaning, morality, or human dignity. Written to help ordinary Christians think clearly about the ideas shaping their world, it became a landmark work, and the basis for an accompanying film series.

title: The God Who Is There
fb_summary: In his first and most influential book, Francis Schaeffer examines the philosophical shift of the twentieth century — the widespread abandonment of belief in absolute truth — and traces its effects through art, music, and theology. Against this drift, Schaeffer sets forth "the God who is there," the personal, infinite Creator revealed in Scripture, arguing that only this God provides a foundation for meaning, morality, and hope. Written to equip ordinary Christians to engage thoughtfully with the ideas of their age, it remains one of the most widely read works of twentieth-century Christian apologetics.

title: The Apostolic Preaching of the Cross
fb_summary: Originally Leon Morris's doctoral dissertation at Cambridge, this modern classic examines the key words the apostles used to describe what Christ accomplished on the cross — redemption, covenant, propitiation, reconciliation, and justification. Tracing each term through its Old Testament background and first-century usage, Morris shows with careful scholarship what the New Testament writers actually meant when they described Jesus's death as paying for sin. Thoroughly evangelical in its conclusions, the book remains a foundational study for anyone wanting a deeper grasp of what the cross achieved.

title: The Gospel According to John
edition_note: Revised edition (NICNT, Eerdmans, 1995); original edition first published 1971.
fb_summary: Leon Morris spent over a decade producing this landmark verse-by-verse commentary on John's Gospel, part of the respected New International Commentary on the New Testament series. Combining careful attention to the Greek text with clear explanation, Morris walks readers through John's account of Jesus's signs, teachings, death, and resurrection, always keeping John's stated purpose in view: "that you may believe that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God, and that believing you may have life in his name." Long regarded as one of the finest evangelical commentaries on any book of the Bible.

title: Give Me This Mountain
edition_note: Modern reprint edition (Christian Focus, 2012); original 1966 publication predates the ISBN system.
fb_summary: Dr. Helen Roseveare's autobiography recounts her calling as a young medical missionary to the Belgian Congo in the 1950s, where she built a hospital and training center from nothing at Ibambi and later Nebobongo. Written with candor and self-deprecating humor, she doesn't hide her own fears, failures, and struggles alongside the joys of seeing patients treated and Congolese believers trained in medicine and faith. It offers a vivid, honest picture of what it costs, and what God provides, when someone gives everything to serve Him in a hard place.

title: Living Sacrifice
edition_note: Modern reprint edition (Christian Focus, retitled 'Living Sacrifice: Willing to be Whittled as an Arrow'); original 1979 publication (Christian Literature Crusade) predates this ISBN.
fb_summary: Drawing on her years as a missionary surgeon in the Congo, including imprisonment and suffering during rebel uprisings, Helen Roseveare reflects here on what it means to offer oneself as a "living sacrifice" to God, as Paul urges in Romans 12. Weaving personal stories together with biblical teaching, she examines areas of pride, self-will, and comfort that God calls believers to surrender, showing sacrifice not as loss but as the path to true joy and usefulness in His service. A searching, honest call to wholehearted discipleship.

title: The Canon of Scripture
fb_summary: How did the sixty-six books of the Bible come to be recognized as Scripture? In The Canon of Scripture, respected scholar F. F. Bruce traces that history from the early church's use of the apostles' writings alongside the Old Testament, through debates over which books belonged, to the eventual widespread consensus reflected in councils like Carthage in AD 397. Rather than a sudden decree, Bruce shows canon recognition as a gradual process guided by apostolic origin and the church's own experience of these books' authority. A clear, scholarly answer to an important question of faith.

title: The New Testament Documents: Are They Reliable?
edition_note: Modern reprint edition (Eerdmans); original 1943 publication (Inter-Varsity Fellowship) predates the ISBN system.
fb_summary: Named one of the books that most shaped modern evangelicalism, F. F. Bruce's concise classic asks a historian's question: can the New Testament documents be trusted? Bruce examines when the Gospels and epistles were written, how the New Testament canon took shape, and how the manuscript evidence for the New Testament compares with that for other ancient texts, concluding it is far stronger. Chapters on archaeology and early non-Christian sources add further support. Written for general readers rather than specialists, it remains a trusted, accessible defense of the New Testament's historical reliability.

title: Paul: Apostle of the Heart Set Free
edition_note: ISBN shown is for the Eerdmans (US) hardcover edition; the original 1977 UK edition was published by Paternoster Press under the title 'Paul: Apostle of the Free Spirit'.
fb_summary: Widely regarded as F. F. Bruce's magnum opus, this substantial study follows the apostle Paul's life in roughly chronological order — his strict Jewish upbringing in Tarsus, his zealous persecution of the church, his dramatic conversion on the Damascus road, and his tireless missionary journeys across the Roman world. Alongside the history, Bruce carefully explains the theology of each of Paul's letters in its original setting, showing how a man once bound by the law was set free by grace to proclaim Christ to the nations. A thorough, scholarly, yet readable portrait of Christianity's most influential missionary.

title: Preaching and Preachers
edition_note: ISBN shown is for the 40th-anniversary Zondervan edition; original 1971 Hodder & Stoughton edition ISBN not independently confirmed. Published during Lloyd-Jones's lifetime, based on his own 1969 lectures at Westminster Theological Seminary.
fb_summary: Based on lectures Martyn Lloyd-Jones gave to seminary students in 1969, this modern classic argues that preaching — not entertainment, program, or method — is the church's most urgent need. Drawing on decades in the pulpit at Westminster Chapel, Lloyd-Jones insists true preaching is "theology coming through a man on fire," dependent above all on the Holy Spirit's power rather than technique. He offers practical counsel on preparing and delivering a sermon, but keeps returning to his central conviction: that faithful preaching of God's Word is what the church needs most in every generation.

title: Studies in the Sermon on the Mount
edition_note: Modern reprint edition (Eerdmans, two-volume set); original 1959-60 publication predates the ISBN system. Adapted by Lloyd-Jones himself for publication from sermons preached 1950-51 at Westminster Chapel — distinct from the many multi-volume sermon series compiled and published posthumously after his 1981 death.
fb_summary: Originally preached as sermons at Westminster Chapel, these studies walk verse by verse through Jesus's Sermon on the Mount in Matthew 5–7. Martyn Lloyd-Jones insists the Sermon is not a code of ethics to strive after by human effort, but a description of the character God produces in those truly born again by His Spirit. With characteristic clarity, he unpacks the Beatitudes and Christ's teaching on prayer, anxiety, judgment, and true righteousness, applying each passage searchingly to everyday Christian life. Decades on, it remains one of the most valued studies of Christ's own words.

title: The Knowledge of the Holy
edition_note: Modern reprint edition (HarperOne, 1978 paperback reissue, still in print); original 1961 publication (Harper & Row) predates general ISBN adoption for this title.
fb_summary: In short, meditative chapters, A. W. Tozer explores the attributes of God — His eternity, holiness, wisdom, and sovereignty — arguing that what we believe about God shapes everything else about our lives and worship. Tozer famously wrote that "what comes into our minds when we think about God is the most important thing about us," and this book is his attempt to correct shallow or mistaken ideas of God with a vision of His true majesty. Simply written yet profound, it has become a beloved devotional classic for cultivating reverence and awe before the living God.

title: The Pursuit of God
edition_note: Modern updated reprint edition (WingSpread Publishers); original 1948 publication (Christian Publications) predates the ISBN system.
fb_summary: A. W. Tozer's 1948 devotional classic calls readers beyond mere belief into a living, hungry pursuit of God himself. Drawing on Scripture and the great saints of church history, Tozer contrasts a comfortable, formal Christianity with the wholehearted longing for God's presence he believed the modern church had lost. Short, prayerful chapters explore themes like the removal of the veil, the speaking voice of God, and restlessness of soul. Still widely read today, it remains a gentle but searching call to seek God, not merely religion, with our whole heart.

title: Baffled to Fight Better
edition_note: Modern reprint edition (Discovery House, 1990); original December 1917 publication (Nile Mission Press, Cairo) predates the ISBN system. Chambers proofread this manuscript himself before his November 1917 death, making it the only book-length prose work he personally prepared for publication — his other well-known titles, including 'My Utmost for His Highest', were posthumously compiled by his widow, Biddy Chambers, from shorthand notes of his talks.
fb_summary: Written in 1917 while Oswald Chambers was serving as a YMCA chaplain to soldiers in Egypt during World War I, this short book works through the book of Job to wrestle honestly with the problem of suffering. Rather than offering easy answers, Chambers walks alongside Job's confusion and complaint, showing how God meets us not with explanations but with himself. Best known today for the devotional My Utmost for His Highest, Chambers here brings the same searching, Christ-centred insight to one of life's hardest questions: why do the righteous suffer?

title: My Utmost for His Highest
edition_note: Modern reprint edition (Barbour Publishing, 2013); compiled by Chambers's widow, Biddy Chambers, from shorthand notes of his oral talks, and first self-published in 1924, seven years after his death.
fb_summary: Compiled by his widow Biddy from shorthand notes she had taken of his oral talks, My Utmost for His Highest was first self-published in 1924, seven years after Oswald Chambers died at forty-three while serving as a YMCA chaplain to troops in Egypt. Arranged as 366 daily readings, it presses readers past religious routine toward wholehearted, moment-by-moment surrender to Christ, insisting that a believer's usefulness rests entirely on grace rather than human ability or achievement. Chambers never saw a page of the finished book, yet it has remained continuously in print for a century and is read daily by believers in dozens of languages.

title: From Pearl Harbor to Calvary
edition_note: Modern reprint edition (eChristian Books, 2011); original 1953 publication predates the ISBN system.
fb_summary: Mitsuo Fuchida led the Japanese air attack on Pearl Harbor in 1941. Years later, moved by the testimony of former POW Jacob DeShazer, one of Doolittle's Raiders who had come to faith in a Japanese prison camp, Fuchida bought a Bible and began reading the Gospels. Reading Christ's words from the cross, 'Father, forgive them,' he came to faith in 1950. This short autobiography tells, in his own words, how the man who led an act of war found peace through the cross and was reconciled with former enemies as brothers in Christ.

title: A Visit to the Indians on the Frontiers of Chile
edition_note: No ISBN found and no confirmed modern reprint edition located; original 1840 publication (Seeley, Burnside & Seeley, London).
fb_summary: In 1840, British naval officer turned missionary Allen Gardiner published this firsthand account of his journey among the indigenous peoples of southern Chile, including the Araucanian and Tehuelche peoples. Gardiner records their customs, language, and way of life, and his hopes for bringing the gospel to them. It was an early step in a missionary calling that would eventually lead him to found what became the South American Mission Society, and ultimately to his death while attempting to reach the peoples of Tierra del Fuego. A window into pioneering, sacrificial missionary vision.

title: Lex, Rex: The Law and the Prince
edition_note: Modern reprint edition (Canon Press, 2020, with introduction by Douglas Wilson); original 1644 publication predates the ISBN system.
fb_summary: Published in 1644 amid the upheavals of the English Civil War, Samuel Rutherford's Lex, Rex ('the law is king') argues that even kings are bound under the law of God and cannot rule by arbitrary will. Rutherford, a Scottish Presbyterian minister and theologian, grounded civil government in covenant and accountability rather than the divine right of unlimited monarchy. Provocative enough in its day to be banned and to nearly cost Rutherford his life, the book became a seedbed for later ideas about limited government and the rule of law that still echo in constitutional thought today.

title: Horae Homileticae
edition_note: Modern reprint edition (Forgotten Books) of a single volume from the original 21-volume set of sermon outlines/commentary on the whole Bible, personally compiled by Simeon and issued serially from 1819, with a complete revised edition in 1832-33; predates the ISBN system.
fb_summary: Charles Simeon, longtime vicar of Holy Trinity Church, Cambridge, spent decades preaching verse by verse through nearly the whole Bible. Horae Homileticae ('Hours of Homiletics') gathers that labour into an enormous multi-volume set of sermon outlines spanning Genesis to Revelation, published in the early 1830s near the end of his life. It was never meant as finished sermons to be read aloud unchanged, but as a preacher's aid, showing how to draw the plain sense of a text out to the glory of Christ. Generations of pastors have mined its pages for faithful, Christ-centred exposition.

title: Holiness: Its Nature, Hindrances, Difficulties, and Roots
edition_note: Modern reprint edition (Banner of Truth, enlarged edition); original 1877 publication predates the ISBN system.
fb_summary: J. C. Ryle, the first Bishop of Liverpool, wrote this 1877 classic to call Victorian Christians back to practical, wholehearted holiness at a time when he felt the subject was being neglected or confused with other doctrines. In plain, direct prose, Ryle explains what holiness is, why it matters, what hinders it, and how it grows in the believer's life as fruit of grace, not a means of earning favour with God. Still widely read, it remains a clear, pastoral guide for anyone wanting to grow in likeness to Christ.

title: Practical Religion
edition_note: Modern reprint edition (Banner of Truth); original 1878 publication predates the ISBN system.
fb_summary: A companion to his classic Holiness, J. C. Ryle's 1878 Practical Religion takes up everyday matters of the Christian life: prayer, Bible reading, the state of the heart, self-inquiry, and zeal. Writing as a pastor to ordinary believers rather than scholars, Ryle is direct and searching, urging readers not merely to hold correct doctrine but to live it out daily by grace. The chapters were originally addresses and papers gathered together, and they retain a warm, plainspoken, sermon-like quality that has kept the book in print for well over a century.

title: Bible Characters
edition_note: Modern single-volume reprint edition (Christian Heritage, 2001) compiling the original six-volume series published 1896-1902; predates the ISBN system.
fb_summary: Scottish preacher Alexander Whyte was known for vivid, searching portraits of biblical figures, and this 1896 collection gathers many of his character studies drawn from both Old and New Testaments. Rather than simply retelling their stories, Whyte probes the strengths, failures, and struggles of figures like Abraham, Jacob, and Peter, using their lives as mirrors for the reader's own soul. His aim throughout is pastoral: to show how God's grace meets flawed, ordinary people, and to press home the gospel through the honest, unflattering detail Scripture itself provides.

title: Bunyan Characters
edition_note: Modern reprint edition (Wipf and Stock, 2000) of the first series; original series published 1893-1908 in four volumes (Fleming H. Revell Co.), predating the ISBN system.
fb_summary: Alexander Whyte turns his gift for character study to John Bunyan's The Pilgrim's Progress in this multi-volume work beginning in 1893, examining figures such as Christian, Mr. Worldly Wiseman, and Giant Despair. Whyte treats Bunyan's allegorical figures as studies in the Christian life, drawing out the spiritual lessons embedded in each character's choices and failings. Written by a Scottish minister deeply shaped by Bunyan's own writing, it serves as both a companion to Pilgrim's Progress and a searching guide to the believer's own pilgrimage toward the Celestial City.

title: Ecce Deus: Essays on the Life and Doctrine of Jesus Christ
edition_note: Modern reprint edition (Forgotten Books, Classic Reprint series); original 1867/1868 publication predates the ISBN system.
fb_summary: Published in 1868, Joseph Parker's Ecce Deus ('Behold God') was written partly as a response to the anonymous and controversial Ecce Homo, which had presented Jesus chiefly in his humanity. Parker, a leading London Congregationalist preacher, works through Christ's birth, teaching, miracles, death, and resurrection to argue for his full deity as God incarnate, not merely an admirable moral teacher. The essays combine careful reasoning with the preacher's warmth Parker was known for, offering readers of his day a robust defence of the historic Christian confession that Jesus is truly God.

title: How to Pray
edition_note: Modern reprint edition (Whitaker House); original 1900 publication predates the ISBN system.
fb_summary: R. A. Torrey, a close associate of D. L. Moody and a leader in the evangelical revivals of his era, wrote this practical 1900 handbook to teach ordinary Christians how to pray effectively. Drawing on Scripture's own teaching about prayer, Torrey covers hindrances to prayer, the conditions for answered prayer, and the importance of persistence and faith. Rather than abstract theory, the book is written as a working guide, meant to be applied rather than merely read, and it remains a widely used introduction to a life of prayer.

title: The Person and Work of the Holy Spirit
edition_note: Modern reprint edition (Zondervan); original 1910 publication predates the ISBN system.
fb_summary: In this 1910 work, R. A. Torrey sets out a systematic study of who the Holy Spirit is and what he does in the life of the believer and the church. Torrey covers the Spirit's deity and personality, his role in conviction, regeneration, sanctification, and empowerment for service, working carefully through relevant Scripture passages rather than personal experience alone. Written at a time of renewed interest in the Spirit's work within evangelical circles, it remains a clear, biblically grounded introduction to a doctrine often misunderstood or neglected.

title: Can the Old Faith Live with the New? Or, the Problem of Evolution and Revelation
edition_note: Modern reprint edition; original 1885 publication (William Blackwood, Edinburgh) predates the ISBN system.
fb_summary: Scottish minister George Matheson, blind from his youth yet a gifted scholar and preacher, published this 1885 work to address one of the great intellectual challenges of his day: could historic Christian faith survive the rise of evolutionary science? Rather than treating faith and science as enemies, Matheson argues that a properly understood Christianity has nothing to fear from evolution, and that new scientific understanding can even deepen appreciation for God's ongoing work in creation. It stands as a thoughtful nineteenth-century attempt to hold fast to Scripture while engaging honestly with new knowledge.

title: Devil at My Heels
edition_note: This is the 2003 memoir co-authored with David Rensin — a distinct work in content from Zamperini's earlier 1956 memoir of the same title, co-authored with Helen Itria, which predates the ISBN system.
fb_summary: Louis Zamperini was an Olympic runner turned World War II bombardier whose plane crashed in the Pacific. He survived 47 days adrift on a life raft, only to be captured and brutally treated in Japanese POW camps. In his own memoir, Zamperini tells this ordeal firsthand, and how years later, at a 1949 Billy Graham crusade in Los Angeles, he came to faith in Christ. That conversion freed him from the anger and nightmares of his past and led him, remarkably, to seek out and forgive his former captors.

title: Born Again
edition_note: ISBN shown is for a current Chosen Books print edition; the exact ISBN of the original 1976 first printing was not independently confirmed.
fb_summary: Charles Colson served as a hard-nosed aide to President Nixon and was imprisoned for his role in the Watergate scandal. In this 1976 memoir, Colson recounts his fall from power, his unexpected conversion to Christianity shortly before pleading guilty, and his experience of prison itself. The book became a bestseller and launched Colson's decades of prison ministry work. Honest about his own pride and failure, it is above all a testimony to the reality of grace reaching even the powerful and the guilty, and reshaping a life from the inside out.

title: Loving God
edition_note: ISBN shown is for a current Zondervan print edition; the exact ISBN of the original 1983 first printing was not independently confirmed.
fb_summary: In this 1983 follow-up to his bestselling memoir Born Again, Chuck Colson turns from his own conversion story to explore what genuine love for God looks like in daily life. Drawing on Scripture and the examples of believers he had come to know, including many in his prison ministry work, Colson argues that loving God is inseparable from obedience, sacrifice, and love for others, not mere sentiment or private feeling. Practical and searching, the book challenges readers to move from admiring faith to living it out.

title: Run Baby Run
edition_note: Modern reprint edition (Bridge-Logos); original 1968 publication (Logos International, co-authored with Jamie Buckingham) predates the ISBN system.
fb_summary: Nicky Cruz grew up amid poverty and violence in Puerto Rico and New York, eventually leading a feared street gang in Brooklyn. His 1968 memoir tells how a chance encounter with street preacher David Wilkerson, the founder of Teen Challenge, led to a dramatic conversion to Christianity that turned his life around. Companion to Wilkerson's own book The Cross and the Switchblade, Run Baby Run became a bestselling testimony of God's power to reach even the most hardened, and Cruz went on to a lifelong ministry to young people caught in gangs and addiction.

title: I Dared to Call Him Father
edition_note: ISBN shown is for the original Chosen Books 1978 hardcover edition (co-authored with Richard H. Schneider).
fb_summary: Bilquis Sheikh was a high-born Pakistani Muslim woman who, after a series of vivid dreams and a growing hunger to know God personally, began quietly reading the Bible. Her 1978 memoir tells how this search led her, at real personal cost within her family and community, to put her faith in Christ and dare to call God 'Father.' Translated into many languages, the book has become a widely read account of one woman's journey from formal religion to a living relationship with God.

title: The First Blast of the Trumpet Against the Monstrous Regiment of Women
edition_note: Modern reprint edition (Kessinger Publishing, Legacy Reprints); original 1558 publication predates the ISBN system.
fb_summary: Written in 1558 while John Knox was in exile on the Continent, this polemical tract argues against female rule, aimed chiefly at the Catholic queens Mary I of England and Mary of Guise, regent of Scotland, whom Knox blamed for persecuting Protestants. The title's 'monstrous regiment' means unnatural governance rather than an army. The pamphlet caused Knox lasting political trouble, notably straining his later relationship with the Protestant Queen Elizabeth I. It remains a striking historical document of Reformation-era political and religious conflict, read today mainly for its historical significance rather than its argument.

title: The History of the Reformation in Scotland
edition_note: Modern reprint edition (Banner of Truth); written by Knox 1559-1566 but not published in full until after his 1572 death (1586-87 first complete printing); predates the ISBN system.
fb_summary: John Knox, the leading figure of the Scottish Reformation, wrote this history of the movement he himself helped lead, from the early stirrings of Protestant conviction in Scotland through the establishment of the reformed Church of Scotland. Published posthumously in 1587, it combines firsthand narrative with documents and letters, offering a vivid, partisan, eyewitness account rather than a detached history. For all its bias, it remains an invaluable primary source for understanding how the Reformation took root in Scotland and shaped the nation's religious life for centuries afterward.

title: The Gospel Worthy of All Acceptation
edition_note: Modern reprint edition (Gale/Sabin Americana reprint of the 1801 second edition); original 1785 publication predates the ISBN system.
fb_summary: Baptist pastor and theologian Andrew Fuller published this influential 1785 work to challenge the 'hyper-Calvinism' common among some Baptist churches of his day, which hesitated to freely offer the gospel to all who heard it. Fuller argued from Scripture that every hearer has a duty to believe in Christ, and that God's sovereign election does not undermine sincere, open invitation to sinners everywhere. The book reshaped Baptist thinking on evangelism and missions, and directly influenced William Carey's pioneering missionary vision not long after its publication.

title: My Life and Sacred Songs
edition_note: ISBN belongs to an AMS Press reprint of the fuller 1907 US edition (titled 'My Life and the Story of the Gospel Hymns and of Sacred Songs and Solos'); the original 1906 UK edition (Hodder & Stoughton/Morgan & Scott) predates the ISBN system. This is Sankey's prose memoir, distinct from any hymnal or hymn collection.
fb_summary: Ira Sankey was the gifted gospel singer and songleader who partnered with evangelist D. L. Moody in revival campaigns across Britain and America. Published in 1906, this memoir tells the story of that partnership and Sankey's own musical calling, along with the background behind many of the hymns and gospel songs he helped popularize, some of which he wrote or compiled himself. It offers a firsthand window into the great transatlantic revival meetings of the late nineteenth century and the role music played in bringing the gospel to vast crowds.

title: Chosen by God
fb_summary: Theologian R. C. Sproul wrote this 1986 book to explain, in clear and accessible terms, the biblical doctrine of election, God's sovereign choice in salvation. Anticipating that the topic can feel troubling or abstract, Sproul works carefully through Scripture to show why he believes it is, rightly understood, a source of comfort and assurance rather than fear. Known for making serious theology understandable to ordinary readers, Sproul presents election not as a cold doctrine but as evidence of God's grace toward those who could never save themselves.

title: Everyone's a Theologian: An Introduction to Systematic Theology
fb_summary: R. C. Sproul's premise in this 2014 introduction is simple: every Christian is already a theologian, whether a good one or a poor one, because everyone holds beliefs about God. Written to make systematic theology accessible to ordinary believers rather than only scholars, the book walks through core Christian doctrines, God, Scripture, humanity, Christ, salvation, the church, and last things, in clear, readable chapters. It serves as a broad, orderly introduction for readers wanting a firmer grasp of what the Bible teaches as a whole.

title: Knowing Scripture
fb_summary: In this 1977 guide, R. C. Sproul addresses why and how ordinary Christians should study the Bible for themselves rather than leaving interpretation to scholars alone. He answers common objections to personal Bible study, then lays out sound, practical principles of interpretation, attending to context, genre, and the analogy of Scripture, so readers can approach difficult passages with confidence rather than guesswork. Clear and down-to-earth, it has long served as an entry point for believers wanting to read their Bibles more carefully and faithfully.

title: The Holiness of God
fb_summary: Widely regarded as R. C. Sproul's signature work, this 1985 book centers on a single, weighty theme: the holiness of God, and what it means for sinful human beings to stand before a perfectly holy God. Through biblical accounts such as Isaiah's vision in the temple and Moses at the burning bush, Sproul explores why holiness provokes both awe and dread, and how the gospel alone makes it possible for sinners to be reconciled to a holy God. It remains one of the most widely read modern treatments of God's character.

title: Christianity and Liberalism
edition_note: Modern reprint edition (Eerdmans, new ed. with foreword by Carl Trueman, 2009); original work published 1923, predates the ISBN system.
fb_summary: Princeton theologian J. Gresham Machen wrote this landmark 1923 work amid growing debate within American Protestantism over theological liberalism. Machen argues that liberal Christianity, in denying core doctrines such as the deity of Christ, his atoning death, and his bodily resurrection, was not a modernized form of Christianity but effectively a different religion altogether, despite retaining Christian vocabulary. Measured and scholarly rather than polemical in tone, the book remains a significant statement of historic Christian orthodoxy and continues to shape conversations about doctrine and church identity today.

title: The Virgin Birth of Christ
edition_note: Modern reprint edition (Baker Books); original work published 1930, predates the ISBN system.
fb_summary: In this detailed 1930 study, J. Gresham Machen defends the biblical doctrine that Jesus Christ was born of a virgin, examining the New Testament accounts in Matthew and Luke alongside early church history and the arguments of contemporary liberal critics who dismissed the doctrine as legend. Written with careful scholarship, the book argues that the virgin birth is well attested and integral to a right understanding of who Christ is. It stands as one of the most thorough conservative defences of the doctrine produced in the twentieth century.

title: What Is Faith?
edition_note: Modern reprint edition (Eerdmans, 1946 reprint still in print); original work published 1925 by Macmillan, predates the ISBN system.
fb_summary: In this 1925 classic, Presbyterian scholar J. Gresham Machen tackles a question every believer eventually asks: what does it actually mean to have faith? Writing against a culture eager to reduce faith to vague feeling, Machen makes the case that real faith is inseparable from truth — trusting in who Christ is and what He has done. Clear, thoughtful, and still widely read today, it's a good companion for anyone wanting firmer footing for what they believe and why.

title: A Book of Sermons
edition_note: Original 1932 self-published edition, per WorldCat; predates the ISBN system (est. 1970). A print-on-demand facsimile exists but no verifiable ISBN was found for it.
fb_summary: Charles Albert Tindley, the son of former slaves who became a beloved Philadelphia pastor and pioneering gospel songwriter (he wrote "We'll Understand It Better By and By"), left behind this 1932 collection of his preaching. Published shortly after his death, with a sketch of his own life story included, the sermons carry the warmth and plain-spoken hope that filled his congregation each week — pointing hearers, whatever their circumstances, toward the comfort and promises of Christ.

title: Through It All
fb_summary: Gospel singer and songwriter Andraé Crouch tells his own story in this 1974 autobiography, written with Nina Ball. From his California upbringing to writing beloved songs like "Through It All" and "My Tribute," Crouch shares how his family's faith and the trials he faced shaped both his music and his walk with Christ. It's an honest, encouraging look at how God can use ordinary struggles for a lasting testimony.

title: Then Sings My Soul
edition_note: Original 1968 F.H. Revell edition; no verifiable ISBN was found despite the ISBN system existing by this date (some early Revell printings were not assigned/catalogued with one).
fb_summary: For decades George Beverly Shea sang before Billy Graham's crusades, his deep voice carrying hymns like "How Great Thou Art" to millions. In this 1968 autobiography, written with Fred Bauer and named for that hymn's refrain, Shea looks back over a life given to music and ministry — from small-town beginnings to stadiums around the world — always returning to the same simple thread: a life devoted to pointing others to Christ.

title: The Pelican Island, and Other Poems
edition_note: Modern facsimile reprint edition (Kessinger Publishing, 2008); original work published 1828, predates the ISBN system.
fb_summary: English hymn writer James Montgomery is best remembered for hymns like "Angels, from the Realms of Glory," but he was also an accomplished poet. "The Pelican Island" (1827), inspired by an explorer's account of an island where pelicans nested and died for generations, is a sweeping blank-verse meditation on creation itself — tracing the natural world from its earliest ages toward the arrival of humanity, and the wonder and moral weight that brings.

title: The Rise and Progress of Religion in the Soul
edition_note: Modern reprint edition (Soli Deo Gloria Publications, 2005); original work published 1745, predates the ISBN system.
fb_summary: Philip Doddridge's 1745 devotional classic walks readers through the whole shape of Christian experience — from spiritual indifference, through conviction and conversion, to a growing life of faith — with a prayer closing each chapter. Its clear, searching honesty about the human heart made it one of the most influential devotional books of the 18th century; it's the book credited with leading William Wilberforce, later famous for his fight against the slave trade, to faith in Christ.

title: Commentary on True and False Religion
edition_note: Modern reprint edition (Wipf & Stock, Latin Works and Correspondence of Huldreich Zwingli series, 2015); original work published 1525, predates the ISBN system.
fb_summary: Swiss reformer Huldrych Zwingli addressed this 1525 work to King Francis I of France, laying out — in twenty-nine chapters — what he believed Scripture actually taught on subjects from baptism and the Lord's Supper to the church and civil authority. It stands as one of the earliest full statements of Reformed theology, written by a man convinced that true religion begins and ends with God's Word rather than human tradition.

title: A Defence of the True and Catholic Doctrine of the Sacrament of the Body and Blood of Christ
edition_note: Modern reprint edition (Wipf & Stock, 2004); original work published 1550, predates the ISBN system. The first full-length book published under Cranmer's own name.
fb_summary: Thomas Cranmer, the Archbishop of Canterbury who helped shape the English Reformation, wrote this 1550 defense of what he believed the Lord's Supper truly is — a remembrance of Christ's finished sacrifice, received by faith, rather than a repeated offering. Written at real personal risk (he would later be martyred for his convictions), it's a careful, Scripture-grounded case for a doctrine he held to the end.

title: Foundation of Christian Doctrine
edition_note: Original work (Dat Fundament des Christelycken leers, 1539-40) predates the ISBN system; ISBN given is for the modern standard scholarly edition, The Complete Writings of Menno Simons (Herald Press, ed. J.C. Wenger, trans. Leonard Verduin), which contains this work in full alongside Menno's other tracts.
fb_summary: Menno Simons, the former Catholic priest whose name became attached to the Mennonite church, wrote this foundational work in 1539 while living as a hunted man for his faith. Covering repentance, faith, baptism, the Lord's Supper, and the character of true ministers, it became the doctrinal anchor for the persecuted Anabaptist communities of his day — a plain, earnest call to build a life, and a church, on nothing but the Word of God.

title: Meditations on the Cross
edition_note: Original 1935 edition (Willett, Clark and Company, trans. Helen Topping and Marion R. Draper); predates the ISBN system, and no verifiable ISBN was found for any modern reprint.
fb_summary: Toyohiko Kagawa, the Japanese Christian who spent years living among the poorest of Kobe's slums, offers a set of devotional reflections on the crucifixion of Christ in this 1935 work. Shaped by his own life of costly compassion, Kagawa's meditations invite readers to sit with the suffering of the cross — not as a distant doctrine, but as the very source of the love that carried him into the slums in the first place.

title: Songs from the Slums
edition_note: Modern reprint edition; original work published 1935 by Cokesbury Press (Nashville), predates the ISBN system.
fb_summary: Written by Toyohiko Kagawa, the Japanese evangelist and social reformer who chose to live among Kobe's poorest residents, this 1935 collection of poems grew directly out of that experience. Rather than looking away from poverty and suffering, Kagawa's verses find Christ present within it — a reminder that the gospel took root, for him, not in comfort but among the very people society had forgotten.

title: A Grammar of the Yoruba Language
edition_note: Original 1843 edition; predates the ISBN system. Later editions and reprints exist but title/date attributions are inconsistent across booksellers (some list a substantially revised 1852 edition under the same title), so no specific reprint ISBN is claimed here.
fb_summary: Samuel Ajayi Crowther, a freed slave who became the first African Anglican bishop, produced this pioneering grammar as part of his life's work translating Scripture into his native Yoruba. Long before he was consecrated bishop, Crowther's careful linguistic scholarship gave the Yoruba-speaking peoples of West Africa their language in print for the first time — laying groundwork so the Bible itself could one day be read in their own tongue.

title: The Life of Rev. William James Hall, M.D.: Medical Missionary to the Slums of New York, Pioneer Missionary to Pyong Yang, Korea
edition_note: Modern print-on-demand facsimile reprint (Forgotten Books); original work published 1897 by Press of Eaton & Mains, predates the ISBN system. A biography of her husband written by Hall herself, not a posthumous compilation by another party.
fb_summary: After her husband William James Hall — a medical missionary who served first in the slums of New York and then in Pyongyang, Korea — died of typhus at just 34, Dr. Rosetta Sherwood Hall wrote this 1897 biography from his letters and their shared story. It's a widow's tribute to a life poured out in healing and gospel witness, and to the faith that carried them both through hardship on the Korean mission field.

title: Abide in Christ
edition_note: Modern reprint edition (Whitaker House, 2002); original work published 1895, predates the ISBN system.
fb_summary: South African pastor Andrew Murray drew on Jesus's words in John 15 — "Abide in me" — to write this beloved set of daily meditations, first published in 1895. Each short reading unpacks what it means to live in constant, moment-by-moment communion with Christ, not as an achievement but as simple, trusting rest in Him. It remains one of the most widely read devotional classics on the Christian life.

title: With Christ in the School of Prayer
edition_note: Modern reprint edition (Whitaker House mass-market paperback); original work published 1885, predates the ISBN system.
fb_summary: In this 1885 devotional classic, Andrew Murray takes readers through a "school" of meditations on prayer, following Christ's own teaching and example in the Gospels. Practical and searching by turns, it treats prayer not as a technique to master but as the natural language of a life abiding in Christ — and has shaped generations of believers' prayer lives since.

title: Waiting on God
edition_note: Modern reprint edition (Whitaker House mass-market paperback); original work published 1896, predates the ISBN system.
fb_summary: Waiting on God, first published in 1896, is a month of daily meditations in which Andrew Murray works through Scripture's repeated call to "wait on the Lord." Writing out of decades of pastoral ministry in South Africa, Murray presses believers away from anxious self-effort and toward quiet, expectant dependence on God's timing and grace, arguing that waiting is not passive resignation but active trust in a Father who is always at work. Alongside Abide in Christ and With Christ in the School of Prayer, it remains one of Murray's most widely read calls to a life of unhurried surrender to Christ.

title: Worship
fb_summary: British worship leader and songwriter Graham Kendrick — known for songs like "Shine, Jesus, Shine" — explores the biblical foundations of corporate worship in this 1984 book. Drawing on Scripture rather than musical trends, Kendrick asks what it really means for God's people to gather and worship together, offering both theological grounding and practical wisdom for church leaders and congregations alike.

title: My Life: A Guided Tour (The Autobiography of Kenneth N. Taylor)
fb_summary: Kenneth N. Taylor created The Living Bible, the plain-language paraphrase that opened Scripture to millions of readers who found older translations hard to follow, and founded Tyndale House Publishers. In this 1991 autobiography, Taylor tells the story behind that work — begun, famously, as a project to help his own ten children understand the Bible during family devotions — and reflects on a life spent making God's Word accessible.

title: Sing!: How Worship Transforms Your Life, Family, and Church
edition_note: Co-authored with Kristyn Getty.
fb_summary: Modern hymn writers Keith and Kristyn Getty — known for "In Christ Alone" and other widely sung congregational songs — make the case in "Sing!" (2017) that corporate singing isn't decoration on top of church life but central to it. Grounded in Scripture and years of leading worship around the world, the book explores how singing together shapes families, churches, and individual believers, and calls readers back to full-voiced, Christ-centered praise.

title: Sing!: How Worship Transforms Your Life, Family, and Church
edition_note: Co-authored with Keith Getty.
fb_summary: Husband-and-wife hymn writers Keith and Kristyn Getty, best known for "In Christ Alone," wrote "Sing!" (2017) to explore why the Bible so consistently calls God's people to sing. Part biblical study, part reflection on their own years of worship leading, the book argues that congregational singing shapes faith at home and in church — and invites readers to recover it as a daily, Christ-centered practice rather than an occasional performance.

title: The Pastor's Wife
edition_note: Original 1970 Hodder & Stoughton edition, prepared with editor Charles Foley from Wurmbrand's own testimony.
fb_summary: Sabina Wurmbrand was married to Romanian pastor Richard Wurmbrand when Communist authorities imprisoned him for his faith; she herself was later sent to a labor camp and left to raise their son alone. In this 1970 memoir, Sabina tells her own side of that story — of forced labor, separation, and grief — and the faith in Christ that sustained her through years most people can scarcely imagine.

title: God's Undertaker: Has Science Buried God?
fb_summary: Oxford mathematician John Lennox tackles one of the most common modern objections to faith head-on: does science leave any room for God? In "God's Undertaker" (2007), Lennox examines evidence from cosmology, biology, and the history of science itself, arguing that far from burying belief in God, real science points toward a Creator. It's a thoughtful resource for anyone wrestling with the perceived conflict between faith and science.

title: Operation World
edition_note: ISBN is for the widely available 6th '21st Century Edition' (2001, co-authored with Jason Mandryk); the original 1974 edition was a 32-page self-published booklet predating this title's common ISBN use.
fb_summary: Missionary Patrick Johnstone first compiled "Operation World" in 1974 as a country-by-country prayer guide to the nations of the world — their peoples, languages, and spiritual needs. What began as a modest handbook became one of the most widely used missions prayer resources anywhere, mobilizing generations of Christians to pray by name for places and peoples they might otherwise never think of.

title: Chasing the Dragon: One Woman's Struggle Against the Darkness of Hong Kong's Drug Dens
edition_note: Current Chosen Books/Baker Publishing Group edition; co-written with Andrew Quicke. Originally published 1980 by Hodder & Stoughton.
fb_summary: As a young Englishwoman, Jackie Pullinger moved into Hong Kong's notorious Walled City to bring the gospel to drug addicts, gang members, and others living on the margins. Her 1980 memoir tells the story of that ministry — the friendships formed, the lives changed, and her conviction that Christ's love and power reach even the most broken places. It remains a widely read account of gospel ministry among the addicted and forgotten.

title: Crack in the Wall: The Life and Death of Kowloon Walled City
edition_note: First published 1989 by Hodder & Stoughton.
fb_summary: Crack in the Wall, published in 1989, is Jackie Pullinger's account of the final years and demolition of Hong Kong's Kowloon Walled City — the ungoverned, Triad-controlled slum where she had lived and ministered among drug addicts and gang members since the 1960s. The book follows the closing chapter of a ministry marked by addicts freed from heroin without the agony normally involved, an outcome Pullinger attributed simply to answered prayer rather than any method of her own. A sequel of sorts to her earlier Chasing the Dragon, it testifies again to a gospel that reaches people written off by everyone else.

title: I Love Idi Amin: The Story of Triumph Under Fire in the Midst of Suffering and Persecution in Uganda
fb_summary: Ugandan Anglican bishop Festo Kivengere lived through the brutal rule of Idi Amin, whose regime killed friends and fellow believers in the church he loved. In this 1977 memoir, Kivengere tells of the suffering the Ugandan church endured — and of his own struggle to forgive, choosing love over hatred toward the man responsible. It's a sobering, hope-filled testimony to what it means to follow Christ under real persecution.

title: An Account of the American Baptist Mission to the Burman Empire: In a Series of Letters, Addressed to a Gentleman in London
edition_note: Modern print-on-demand facsimile reprint (Forgotten Books); original work published 1823 in London by J. Butterworth & Son, predates the ISBN system.
fb_summary: Ann Judson, one of the first American women to serve as an overseas missionary, wrote this 1823 account as a series of letters describing her and her husband Adoniram's pioneering Baptist mission to Burma. Amid disease, imprisonment, and war, the Judsons persisted in translating Scripture and sharing the gospel; Ann's own letters give a firsthand window into the cost — and the faith — behind that early missionary work.

title: The Restraining Hand: Captivity for Christ in China
edition_note: First published London, 1936 (reissued in revised form as 'The Guiding Hand' in 1973); predates the ISBN system. No verifiable ISBN was located for any print edition via WorldCat, AbeBooks, or Open Library — only out-of-print/antiquarian copies were found.
fb_summary: Swiss missionary Rudolf Bosshardt was captured by Communist Red Army soldiers in China in 1934 and forced to march alongside them for 560 days — roughly 2,500 miles — during what became known as the Long March. His 1936 memoir tells that extraordinary story, and explains its title: Bosshardt's conviction that even in captivity, God's hand had restrained his captors and preserved his life for a purpose.

title: God's Dealings with Dora Yu: A Chinese Messenger of the Cross
edition_note: First published Shanghai: Mission Book Co., 1916 (reissued London: Morgan & Scott, 1928); predates the ISBN system. No verifiable ISBN was located via WorldCat or Open Library.
fb_summary: Dora Yu was a Chinese evangelist who trained in Western medicine before giving her life to preaching and teaching, eventually founding a Bible school in Shanghai that helped train leaders for the Chinese church's twentieth-century revival. This 1916 book is her own testimony, told in her own words, of how God worked in her life — a firsthand account from one of the era's most significant Chinese Christian voices.

title: Hymns for Little Children
edition_note: Modern facsimile reprint edition (Leopold Classic Library); original work first published 1848, predates the ISBN system.
fb_summary: In 1848 Cecil Frances Alexander published Hymns for Little Children, written to walk children clause by clause through the Apostles' Creed. From its pages came three hymns still sung around the world: 'All Things Bright and Beautiful,' on God as maker of heaven and earth; 'Once in Royal David's City,' on Christ's birth of the Virgin Mary; and 'There Is a Green Hill Far Away,' on his suffering under Pontius Pilate. Written for the youngest singers, the collection's plain, memorable verses were designed to fix a child's imagination on who Christ is and what he did for them, long before they could grasp a line of formal doctrine.

title: The Invalid's Hymn Book
edition_note: Compiled by Harriett Kiernan; taken over, anonymously expanded, and edited by Elliott from the first edition (1834) onward, growing to over 100 of her own hymns by the sixth edition (1854). Predates the ISBN system — no verifiable ISBN was located for any print edition.
fb_summary: The Invalid's Hymn Book was compiled by Charlotte Elliott's friend Harriett Kiernan for the many chronically ill and housebound believers of their day, and first published in 1834. When illness left Elliott herself largely confined to her room from 1829 onward, she took over its editorship, anonymously enlarging it edition after edition until well over a hundred of her own hymns were included by the 1850s. The hymnal stands as a quiet testimony that a bedridden believer could still serve Christ fully through words offered to others in the same condition.

title: The Fundamentals
edition_note: Modern one-volume reprint edition (Baker Book House, 'A Testimony to the Truth'); original work co-edited by Torrey with A.C. Dixon and, later, Louis Meyer and James Orr, published as a 12-volume series 1910-1915 by the Testimony Publishing Company, Chicago, predates the ISBN system.
fb_summary: The Fundamentals was a series of essays commissioned and co-edited by R.A. Torrey, published between 1910 and 1915 and distributed free to hundreds of thousands of pastors, missionaries, and Christian workers worldwide. Written by nearly a hundred contributors, including B.B. Warfield, James Orr, and Bishop H.C.G. Ryle among others, the essays defended the deity of Christ, the authority and reliability of Scripture, and salvation through Christ's atoning death against the rising theological liberalism of the day. The project gave the word 'fundamentalist' its name and stands as a landmark defense of historic Christian doctrine written by many hands united around one gospel.

title: I Was a Prisoner of Japan
edition_note: Testimony tract distributed by the Bible Meditation League, as told to Don R. Falkenberg; widely reprinted and translated. Predates the ISBN system — no verifiable ISBN was located for any printing.
fb_summary: I Was a Prisoner of Japan is Jacob DeShazer's own account, told to Don Falkenberg of the Bible Meditation League, of his capture after the 1942 Doolittle Raid, forty months of brutal Japanese captivity, and the faith in Christ he found reading a borrowed Bible in his cell. Distributed as a tract across Japan after the war, it reached former Pearl Harbor commander Mitsuo Fuchida, who later credited it as part of what led him to Christ — turning two former enemies into fellow evangelists. Short and unadorned, it remains a vivid testimony to grace reaching even a captor's prison.
